_README: Interpretive text for the public leaderboard page. THESE ARE CLAIMS ABOUT RESULTS — they are only true of the runs named below. export_site.py hard-fails if for_dataset_version / for_config_hash do not match the sweep being exported, and cross-checks band_separated + floor_note against the actual data. If a re-run changes the picture, rewrite this file; do not loosen the checks.

headline_findings.label: Best on-device score
headline_findings.detail: Qwen3.5-4B answers 49 of 53 retrieval-required questions correctly — within statistical noise of a frontier cloud model run through the identical search loop.

headline_findings.label: Same models, no search
headline_findings.detail: With tools switched off, those questions collapse to zero. Every point on this board is retrieval, not memorised knowledge.

headline_findings.label: Easy vs hard retrieval
headline_findings.detail: Small models match frontier models on everyday lookups, then fall away on questions needing several sources or a second search. That gap is the real story.

headline_findings.label: Models that can't search
headline_findings.detail: Five candidates never execute a single tool call, whatever the question. This is a harder gate than model size.

config_notes.provider: Brave is the recommended default: it beat Tavily during configuration screening and used fewer prompt tokens on most models. Tavily remains fully supported.
config_notes.result_format: Markdown-formatted results beat the shipped labelled blocks; JSON and compact numbered lists were both worse and were eliminated.
config_notes.tool_desc: Enriched tool descriptions (what the tool is for, plus a hint to write short keyword queries) were the largest single effect seen in screening.
config_notes.result_count: Five results is the shipped default and nothing beat it. Note that asking for more can be a no-op: the result menu is capped by a token ceiling that silently drops trailing hits.
config_notes.snippet_chars: 280-character snippets retained. Shorter truncation lost information; full-length snippets cost many more tokens for no gain.
config_notes.read_url_policy: Page reading stays available but earns its keep rarely — snippets answer most questions, and models call read_url on well under half a page per question.
config_notes.max_turns: A five-turn budget is not the binding constraint: the winning configurations average under two turns per question.
config_notes.system_prompt: Shipped grounding line retained. A prescriptive 'strategy' prompt raised accuracy but triggered needless searches, and pairing it with markdown was worse than either alone.

metric_definitions.label: Fresh ✓
metric_definitions.description: Share of retrieval-required questions answered correctly. These ask about events after the models' training data, so they cannot be answered from memory. This is the primary score.

metric_definitions.label: Stable ✓
metric_definitions.description: Share of timeless general-knowledge questions answered correctly. Checks that adding web search does not damage what a model already knew.

metric_definitions.label: Searches when needed
metric_definitions.description: How often the model actually searches on a question that requires it. The dominant failure mode for small models is not searching at all.

metric_definitions.label: Needless searches
metric_definitions.description: How often the model searches on requests that need no web access, such as writing a haiku or doing arithmetic. Wasted latency and API calls.

metric_definitions.label: Valid tool calls
metric_definitions.description: Share of the model's tool calls that are well-formed and executable. A model that cannot produce valid calls cannot search at all.

metric_definitions.label: Turns
metric_definitions.description: Average round trips through the search loop per question. On a phone each turn is a full prompt re-processing, so turns dominate response time.

metric_definitions.label: Prompt size
metric_definitions.description: Average tokens sent to the model per question, summed across the loop. Search results and fetched pages dominate it. On a phone this is the number that sets how long an answer takes and how much context is left for the answer itself.

gate_failures.title: Structural — the model is never shown the tools
gate_failures.mechanism: These models ship chat templates that never mention tools. The runtime decides a model supports tool calling by probe-rendering its template, so a template that ignores tools means the tool definitions are never written into the prompt at all, and the parser installed can never emit a tool call. Gemma-3 fails visibly: it tries to search, improvises Google's own tool_code syntax, and guesses the function name wrong — proof it never saw a signature. Hermes-3 fails silently and worse: it fabricates an answer with plausible-looking citations. The fix is a tool-declaring chat template, not a text parser.

gate_failures.title: Compliance — the tools are declared, the model still refuses
gate_failures.mechanism: These templates do declare tools, so the model genuinely sees the schemas — and still never calls them. Phi-4-mini states outright that it cannot browse the web and answers from stale memory instead. This is an alignment prior overriding the tools that are visibly in front of it, and it needs a different lever than a template fix.

limitations: Questions are single-fact and English-only, drawn from one eight-week news window. A model tuned on other topics or languages could rank differently.
Correctness is graded by an LLM judge against a human-verified gold answer. The judge was validated against hand-labelled samples, but it is not a human grader.
The configuration was tuned on an earlier question set and then frozen. These model scores all use that same frozen setup; they are not each model's best possible score.
Quality is measured on a workstation running the same quantised model files a phone would run. Battery, heat and on-device latency are not measured; turns and prompt size are the stand-ins.
Fresh questions go stale by design. Scores are only comparable within one dataset version, which is why the version is stamped on this page.
Each question is asked once, so differences of a few points between neighbouring models are not resolved.
band_note: This board does resolve an order. Harder questions — ones needing several sources, or a second search that depends on the first — separate the models that everyday lookups could not: the top on-device model is now clearly ahead of the bottom of the working band. Neighbouring rows still sit within noise of each other, so read the groupings rather than the exact positions.
config_lift_note: Configuration is worth as much as model choice at this size. Tuning it — richer tool descriptions, Brave, markdown-formatted results — lifted pooled correctness from 0.79 to 0.92 during screening, and nearly doubled the weakest model's score simply by getting it to search at all.
